      JobCount = job_count StartTime = start_time EndTime = end_time
      ErrorMessage = error_message CreatedBy = created_by CreatedAt = created_at
      LastChangedAt = last_changed_at ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD lock.
    
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD StartProcessing.
    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      " 1. Mark status as 'INPR' immediately in the database
      UPDATE zaf_dt_backjob SET status = 'INPR' WHERE id = _key-id.

      " 2. Add to global job queue for the SAVE phase
      INSERT ls_key-id INTO TABLE gt_jobs.
    ENDLOOP.

    result = VALUE #( FOR key IN keys ( Id = key-id %param = CORRESPONDING #( key ) ) ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD SetInitialStatus.
    LOOP AT keys INTO DATA(ls_key).
      SELECT SINGLE * FROM zaf_dt_backjob WHERE id = _key-id INTO (ls_db).
      IF ls_db-status IS INITIAL.
        ls_db-status = 'NEW'.
        APPEND ls_db TO mt_buffer_upsert.
      ENDIF.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

" --- Saver Class Implementation ---
CLASS lsc_zi_af_backjob DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver.
  PROTECTED SECTION.
    METHODS finalize REDEFINITION.
    METHODS check_before_save REDEFINITION.
    METHODS save REDEFINITION.
    METHODS cleanup REDEFINITION.
    METHODS cleanup_finalize REDEFINITION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS lsc_zi_af_backjob IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD finalize.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD check_before_save.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD save.
    " 1. Standard DB Persistence
    IF lhc_zi_af_backjob=>mt_buffer_delete IS NOT INITIAL.
      DELETE zaf_dt_backjob FROM TABLE @lhc_zi_af_backjob=>mt_buffer_delete.
    ENDIF.

    IF lhc_zi_af_backjob=>mt_buffer_upsert IS NOT INITIAL.
      MODIFY zaf_dt_backjob FROM TABLE @lhc_zi_af_backjob=>mt_buffer_upsert.
    ENDIF.

    " 2. Trigger Async logic using the reference pattern
    LOOP AT lhc_zi_af_backjob=>gt_jobs INTO DATA(lv_id).
      CALL FUNCTION 'ZAF_FM_BACKJOB'
        IN BACKGROUND TASK
        DESTINATION 'NONE'  " Critical for separate LUW execution
        EXPORTING
          iv_id = lv_id.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD cleanup.
    " Clear all buffers after the LUW is complete
    CLEAR: lhc_zi_af_backjob=>mt_buffer_upsert,
           lhc_zi_af_backjob=>mt_buffer_delete,
           lhc_zi_af_backjob=>gt_jobs.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD cleanup_finalize.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

Step 7: Create behavior definition on Consumption view

projection;
strict ( 2 );
use draft;

define behavior for zc_af_backjob //alias <alias_name>
{
  use create;
  use update;
  use delete;

  use action StartProcessing;

  use action activate;
  use action resume;
  use action Edit;
  use action Discard;
  use action Prepare;
}

Step 8: Create service Definition on top of Consumption view

@EndUserText.label: 'service defination for background job'
define service Zsevd_bg_job {
  expose zc_af_backjob;
}

Step 9: Create service binding on top of service defination

Step 10: Create Function Module

FUNCTION zaf_fm_backjob.
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
*"  IMPORTING VALUE(IV_ID) TYPE ZAF_DT_BACKJOB-ID
*"----------------------------------------------------------------------
  DATA: lv_jobname   TYPE tbtcjob-jobname,
        lv_jobcount  TYPE tbtcjob-jobcount,
        lv_timestamp TYPE timestampl.

  " 1. Dynamic Job Name for better monitoring in SM37
  lv_jobname = |ZAF_BG_{ iv_id+0(8) }|.

  CALL FUNCTION 'JOB_OPEN'
    EXPORTING jobname = lv_jobname
    IMPORTING jobcount = lv_jobcount.

  IF sy-subrc = 0.
    GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_timestamp.

    " 2. Update DB with system metadata immediately
    UPDATE zaf_dt_backjob
      SET job_name   = _jobname,
          job_count  = _jobcount,
          status     = 'INPR',
          start_time = _timestamp
      WHERE id = _id.

    " 3. Push data to UI so it's visible while report processes
    COMMIT WORK.
  ENDIF.

  " 4. Schedule the report
  SUBMIT zaf_bg_job_report WITH p_id = iv_id
    VIA JOB lv_jobname NUMBER lv_jobcount AND RETURN.

  " 5. Release job
  CALL FUNCTION 'JOB_CLOSE'
    EXPORTING jobname = lv_jobname jobcount = lv_jobcount strtimmed = 'X'.
ENDFUNCTION.

Step 11: Create a Report Program

REPORT zaf_bg_job_report.

PARAMETERS: p_id TYPE zaf_dt_backjob-id.

DATA: lv_timestamp TYPE timestampl.

START-OF-SELECTION.

  IF p_id IS INITIAL.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

  WAIT UP TO 5 SECONDS.

  GET TIME STAMP FIELD lv_timestamp.

  " Update final status to match the reference 'DONE'
  UPDATE zaf_dt_backjob
    SET status        = 'DONE',
        end_time      = _timestamp,
        error_message = ''
    WHERE id = @p_id.

  COMMIT WORK.

Result :

Select Multiple Records then click Action Button Start Job.

 

 

Conclusion :

This approach demonstrates how the SAP ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) can be extended beyond standard transactional processing to meet demanding real-world requirements. By leveraging the Saver Class to trigger asynchronous background jobs, you ensure that resource-intensive operations—such as bulk status updates in SD or MM—are executed efficiently without affecting the performance of the main transaction.

entry.title: From Idea to App in Minutes: Generating SAP Fiori Elements Applications with the Project Accelerator
entry.content:
The SAP Fiori tools Project Accelerator is an AI-assisted capability that transforms business requirements provided as text, image, or a combination of both into a SAP Fiori elements application. The generated application can be backed by either the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) or ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP).

The Project Accelerator automates the generation of data models, services, UI scaffolding, and mock data, enabling you to quickly turn ideas from brainstorming or whiteboarding sessions into tangible applications that can be immediately previewed and iterated upon.

 

Where it fits in the SAP Fiori Tools ecosystem

The Project Accelerator is built directly into SAP Fiori tools and can be used within SAP BAS.

It complements existing SAP Fiori tools capabilities such as project generation, application preview, and annotation editing. By generating initial project artifacts directly from business requirements, the Project Accelerator helps you quickly establish a solid project structure.

 

What the Project Accelerator Generates

Depending on the selected programming model, the Project Accelerator generates the following artifacts:

CAP:

For CAP-based applications, the Project Accelerator generates:

  • An SAP Fiori elements application
  • Data models and services
  • Local mock data

 

RAP :

For RAP-based applications, the Project Accelerator generates:

  • An SAP Fiori elements application
  • A RAP back-end artifact
  • An OData V4 service endpoint
  • Local mock data

 

Important note: Business logic is not automatically generated in either CAP or RAP projects. Developers must implement the application logic manually after the initial scaffolding is created.

 

 

Step-by-step guide for the RAP Accelerator Through Business Application Studio (BAS)

In this guide, we will generate an SAP Fiori elements app for managing sports games using the RAP Accelerator with image input. This app will allow users to view game schedules, team information, and game details. Later, we will modify the application to target soccer.

 

 

 

1) Prepare your input

Before starting the generation process, gather your business requirements, which are the inputs you prove to the Project Accelerator.

The Input can be in the following formats:

 

Text

You can write a clear description of your application, including:

  • The purpose of the app
  • Entities and their relationships
  • Fields and properties
  • Labels and restrictions
  • Desired UI layout

Tip: Field values, logic, and restrictions are usually easier to describe using text.

 

Images

You can upload images to describe the desired structure or layout, such as:

  • A data model diagram
  • A UI mockup
  • A List Report layout showing columns
  • Object Page sections or tables
  • Screenshots of an existing application

Tip: Layouts and visual structures are easier to describe using images.

 

I'll use the following image to build a sports game management scenario. Users will be able to see which teams are playing against each other, the league and age group, and where and when the game takes place. The app will also display team details, including the trainer and players.

 

Figure 1. Database model for sports game management scenario

When I first generated the application without specifying a sport type, the underlying LLM inferred the context and generated soccer-related mock data. To see how changes to the input such as specifying the sport type affects the output, I regenerated the application with several modifications to the original image. The results, shown in the Appendix , demonstrate how the AI adapts the generated data based on more specific input.

 

Using Multimedia Input

It is important to keep in mind that the accelerator accepts a single file as input. If you want to upload both text and images, or multiple images, you should first combine them into a single document, either as a word document file (.docx) or as a markdown (.md) file and later upload the file to the accelerator as the business requirement.

If you are using a .md file, you should also upload the images to your workspace folder.

 

 

2) Set up your environment:

To access the Project Accelerator, you need to be in Dev Space with the necessary extensions installed.

Prerequisites

  1. You need to have access to SAP Build Code (What is SAP Build Code | SAP Help Portal)  or signed in for (SAP Build Code Test Drive | SAP Tutorials)
  2. You need to use SAP Business Application Studio as your integrated development environment

 

Required Extensions

Based on your programming model, ensure your Dev Space includes the appropriate extensions:

(If you are creating a new Dev Space, you can select an application type that includes the extensions you need: creating a new Dev Space.)

 

For CAP:

Dev Space must include:

  • CAP Tools
  • SAP Fiori Tools

 

For RAP

Dev must include:

  • SAP Fiori Tools

 

RAP Backend Requirements:

For RAP projects, ensure the following prerequisites are met:

  • RAP backend should be version 2602 or higher
  • You should be connected to the backend system by configuring the destination in SAP Business Application Studio
  • S_DEVELOP authorization

 

Creating a Dev Space

Now, moving on with the creation of a Dev Space:

  1.  Open SAP Build
    1. Click the icon with small squares on the top-right corner
    2. Select Dev Space ManagerFigure 2. How to access the Dev Space Manager
  2. Create a New Dev Space
    • Enter a name for your Dev Space
    • Select the application type 
      • Each application type includes a predefined set of extensions.
      • Select the application type based on the extensions it provides and whether they are required for your programming model or not.
      • For this example, I selected the Full-Stack ABAP Application.

         

    • Click on “Create Dev Space” Figure 3. How to create a Dev Space
  3. After creating your dev space, you will be redirected to the Dev Space Manager, where you can see the newly created dev space and access any other dev spaces you may have created.Figure 4. Dev Space with status "starting"
  4. Wait until the status changes to "Running"
  5. Click on the Dev Space name to start developing

Image 5. Dev Space with status "running"

 

3)  Launching the Project Accelerator

Once you are in your Dev Space, you can launch the Project Accelerator in multiple ways. One of them being:

Using the Template Wizard

  1. Choose “New Project from Template”Figure 6. Step 1 of launching the Project Accelerator
  2. Select “SAP Fiori Generator" and click "Start"Figure 7 . Step 2 of launching the Project Accelerator
  3. On the next screen, click on the “Project Accelerator” to begin 

Figure 8. Step 3 of launching the Project Accelerator

 

 

4) Choose your programming model accelerator

When using the Project Accelerator, you can either select CAP Accelerator or RAP Accelerator tab depending on your programming model.

 

RAP Configuration

For RAP projects, you must:

  1. Specify the Destination:
    1. Select a destination system from the dropdown
    2. Verify your user authentication (user and password for the backend system)
      • OAuth2 authentication can also be configured via the SAP BTP CockpitFigure 9. Accessing the RAP Accelerator
  2. Configure Backend Connection 

 After successful authentication, additional fields will appear:

Here, you should specify your SAPUI5 repository, the package for RAP backend artifact creation, and the transport request if it is required.

Figure 10. Configuring the backend connection

 

 For more information regarding these fields, please refer to: Generating an Application Using an Image | SAP Help Portal

 

 

5)   Enter Your Business Requirements

After choosing your accelerator and specifying your destination, you can upload the input prepared in Step 1 into the business requirements field.

You can either:

  • Upload files to the workspace and then select them using "Choose File" under the business requirements input area
  • Drag and drop documents directly from the file explorer

Hold the Shift key while dragging

Figure 11. Entering the Business Requirements

 

Note: The clearer and more structured your input is, the better the generated result will be.

 

 

6)   Generating the Project

Note: By default, the application is generated in the currently open folder. If you want the project to be created in a different location, you can specify a Project Folder Path before starting the generation.

  1. Click Generate below the input field:
    • The tool will interpret your requirements and generate the project artifacts.Figure 12. Generating the project
    • The interface switches to a generation view displaying progress. Generation may take some time depending on the complexity of your requirementsFigure 13. Generation view

       

    • Once the generation is complete, the Application Information page will appear.Figure 14. Application Information page

       

    • If the project is not in a workspace folder, BAS may display a dialog asking whether to add it after the generation is complete.Figure 15. Adding the project into a workspace

      In this case, you can choose either:

      • “Open Folder”, which creates a new folder for the project, or
      • “Add Project to Workplace”, adding the project to the existing folder. Selecting one of these options adds the project to the workspace and lands you to the Application Information page.

 

 

7)   Preview of the Application

To preview the application:

  1. Select “Preview Application”Figure 16. Previewing the application
  2. Choose “Start Fiori Run” from the context menu
  3. This launches the generated UI.Figure 17. Preview of the application

     

Note: At this stage, the preview only shows the application structure, without any data.

 

Edge Case: Running the Preview from the ABAP Backend

Make sure that you run the preview directly from SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) when previewing the generated application.

If you launch the preview from the ABAP backend instead of BAS, the UI will not render correctly. This is expected behavior as the Project Accelerator stores UI annotations in the local “annotation.xm” file of your Fiori project, not in backend Metadata Extensions. Therefore, the backend cannot render the UI properly without these local annotations.

If the preview is executed from the ABAP backend, the result will appear similar to the following:

Figure 18. Preview of the application when rendered from the ABAP backend example 1

Figure 19. Preview of the application when rendered from the ABAP backend example 2

 

 

8 ) Generate and Preview with Local Mock Data

You can also preview the application with locally generated mock data:

  1. Open the terminal
  2. Enter Ctrl + C to terminate the current process
  3. Select “Preview Application”
  4. Choose “Start-Mock Fiori Run” from the context menuFigure 20. Previewing the application with locally generated mock data
  5. After restarting, you may need to log in again for system authorization.
  6. The UI will now display generated mock data, allowing you to:

Figure 21. Preview of the application with mock dataFigure 22. Preview of the application with mock data - game object page

Figure 23. Preview of the application with mock data - team object page

 

 

9)   Review and Refine

After generation, review the application and refine the configuration as needed. You can adjust field names, labels, page sections, and UI layout using the Page Map:

  1. Click on "Open Page Map"Figure 24. Navigating to Page Map 
  2. Select the pencil icon next to the location you want to modify 
  3. Make your changes.
  4. The preview will automatically refresh and update

 

Example 1: Editing Column Names

Since the business requirement was for general sports, and soccer uses fields (not courts), let's change the column name from "court" to "field."

  • Scenario: Change the field name “Home Court” to “Home Field”
  1. Click on Open Page Map
  2. Click on the pencil icon of “Teams Object Page”Figure 25. Navigating to "Teams" Object Page
  3. Expand the “Table” section
  4. Expand the “Columns” subsection
  5. Click on the “Home Court” field
  6. On the right-hand side (RHS), find the “Label” field and change it to “Home Field”Figure 26. How to change the field name
  7. The preview will automatically refresh and update

Figure 27. Changing the field name in "Team" Object Page

 

Note: Renaming the column in the Teams Object Page only changes how it appears on the Teams Object Page. The “Home Court” column on the Games Object Page won’t update automatically:

Figure 28. Display of unchanged field name in "Game" Object Page

To change it on the Games Object Page as well, you’ll need to rename the column in the Game Object Page. You can do this just by repeating the steps above for Games Object Page.

 

Example 2: Adding a Description to an Object Page

  •  Scenario: Add the description “Game Information” to the Game Object Page.Figure 29. Empty description space in "Game" Object Page
  1. Click on Open Page Map
  2. Select “Games Object Page”Figure 30. Navigating to "Game" Object Page
  3. Click on “Header”.
  4. On the RHS, find Description Type and select “Text” from the dropdown.Figure 31. Navigating to Description Type dropdown

Choosing “Text” allows you to enter custom free text. Alternatively, you can select “Property” to pick from predefined fields or objects.

 In this case, I selected the “Text” option because “Game Information” is not a predefined feature of any existing object in the application.

5. Type “Game Information” into the Description field.

Figure 32. How to add a text to the description space

6. The UI will automatically refresh and update

Figure 33. Preview of "Game" Object Page with description text

 

Example 3: Adding an Icon to an Object Page

  • Scenario: Adding a soccer icon to the Game object page
  1. Click on Open Page Map
  2. Select the “Games Object Page”
  3. Find the icon sectionFigure 34. Navigating to the Icon Explorer
  4. Click the link to the Icon Explorer
  5. Search for “soccer”Image 35. View of the Icon Explorer and searching for the term "soccer"You can also browse the available icons and select the one that fits your goalsFigure 36. Overview of the existing SAPUI5-supported icons
  6. Copy the soccer ball icon’s URL

Figure 37. Copying the soccer ball icon's URL

7. Switch to your Dev Space and paste the URL into the icon fieldFigure 38. Pasting the copied URL into the Icon field8. The UI will refresh automatically and display the iconFigure 39. Preview of the "Game" Object Page with the soccer ball icon

 

Next steps:

After you are satisfied with the UI Layout;

  • For CAP: Add business logic in service handlers; enrich annotations; refine data model.
  • RAP: Implement behavior logic, validation, determinations; adjust annotations; refine UI.

For more information:

  • Official documentation page:

Generating an Application with the Project Accelerator Using SAP Fiori Tools AI | SAP Help Portal

 

Appendix

1)    Specifying the sport type as volleyball


 To see how specifying the sport type affects the generated mock data, I added additional requirements database model I originally used:Figure 40. Updated database model, sport type specified as volleyball

The generated mock data became more volleyball-oriented, as reflected in the “Team” and “Place” names:

Figure 41. Preview with volleyball-specific mock data

I was also interested to see if the number of players would change based on the specified sport type, for example:

  • a volleyball team having at least 6 players
  • a soccer team having 11
  • a basketball team having 5

However, the number of players did not change compared to the original input.

Figure 42. "Team" Object Page displaying players

 If you want to have specific number of instances, you can explicitly include this in your business requirements:Figure 43. Data model diagram with amount of team members specified

Figure 44. "Team" Object Page with 6 players

 

2)    Using Mock UI

For the Mock UI, I uploaded an image intended to represent the list of all games and the corresponding object page:

Figure 45. Mock UI - First attempt

However, the LLM did not recognize this image as one of its supported input types and returned an error:

If you are providing a Mock UI, the input should be clear and focused to avoid such errors.

Figure 46. Error message for unsupported images

In my experience, Mock UIs produce the best results when the input is as simple and clear as possible. For example, the image below worked well:

Figure 47. Mock UI - Second attempt

The Project Accelerator successfully generated the list report and even added “Players” and “Teams” objects to the object page, which was a positive surprise:

Figure 48. Successfully generated application with Mock UIImage 49. Successfully generated application with Mock UI - Object Page

 

3)    Using Multimedia Input: Multiple images

I combined the input material into a single .docx document and uploaded it to the accelerator:Figure 50. The two images, combined in a single document

You can upload the .docx or .md files the same way as you would for images.

The following application was generated:Figure 51. Preview of the application generated with multi-media input

Figure 52. Preview of the application generated with multi-media input - Object Page

 

Mismatch in object fields  

When combining multiple input sources, such as database tables and Mock UI images, it is important that these models are consistent. Inconsistencies between the data model and the UI definition can lead to incorrect or incomplete application generation:

Figure 53. Multi-media input with mismatching fields

Figure 54. Preview of the application generated with the input shown in Figure 53

 The following issue occurred due to such a mismatch:

  • The “Game” entity contains additional fields in the database model that are not represented in the UI.
  • The “Team” entity is missing a role field in the database model that is expected by the UI.

entry.title: Handling Dynamic Fields in SAP RAP using JSON Payload in CDS Custom Entity
entry.content:
Introduction

Core Data Services (CDS) entities in the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) are usually constructed using statically set parameters. Although this guarantees robust typing and clarity, it becomes restrictive when programs have to deal with dynamic inputs like changing fields, tables, or runtime-driven filters.

Such adaptability is crucial in many real-world situations. Nevertheless, dynamic parameter structures are not supported by CDS natively, which frequently results in complicated and difficult-to-maintain designs.

This blog describes an adaptable method that passes dynamic field data as a single parameter and processes it at the ABAP layer using a JSON payload inside a CDS Custom Entity. This keeps the CDS design straightforward while enabling more flexible and scalable RAP solutions.

What are CDS Custom Entities in RAP?

A CDS Custom Entity is a unique kind of CDS entity that does not read data directly from the database in the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). Rather, an ABAP class is used to accomplish it, and the developer has complete control over the data retrieval mechanism.

In contrast to Standard CDS Views:

  • Custom Entities enable ABAP's dynamic data processing.
  • When logic cannot be managed only at the database level, they are usually employed.
  • They offer the freedom to execute intricate or unconventional scenarios, including external integrations or dynamic queries.

Because the data is fetched and processed in the ABAP layer, Custom Entities are well-suited for scenarios where:

  • Input parameters need to be interpreted dynamically
  • Data sources or structures vary at runtime
  • Standard CDS capabilities are not sufficient

Real-Time Business Scenario: Dynamic Field Validation

In some SAP applications, there is a requirement to validate fields dynamically based on user input.

For instance, a user or an outside system might submit a request such as:

  • Verify if a table contains the specified fields.
  • During runtime, dynamically provide field values
  • Tables and fields are not predetermined.

One Sample input would be:

  • Table: SFLIGHT
  • Fields: FLDATE and CARRID
  • Values: LH, 20260313 

The table and fields can be entirely different in a different request. This means that any table and any field must be handled dynamically by the application without the need for preset parameters.

The Problem

If we attempt to use normal CDS to implement this:

  • Each field would require its own set of parameters.
  • As fields alter, the number of parameters would increase.
  • Unknown or dynamic fields cannot be handled.

The Restrictions:
Flexible structures like a "list of fields" cannot be dynamically accepted by CDS. Because of this, the design is inflexible and challenging to scale.

The Solution

We use a single argument (FieldValuesJson) to deliver all field details as a JSON payload in order to resolve this.

There are other entries in this JSON, such as:

  • Table name
  • field name
  • field value

In the ABAP Implementation:

  • An internal table is created by parsing the JSON.
  • Every field is dynamically verified using the Data Dictionary (DD03L).
  • The existence of the field is indicated by the result that is returned.

Without altering the CDS definition, this method enables the application to dynamically handle any table and any field.

Implementation Using CDS Custom Entity

Step 1: Create CDS Custom Entity

First, define a CDS Custom Entity to accept input parameter and return results.

@EndUserText.label: 'Dynamic Field Filter Custom Entity'
@ObjectModel.query.implementedBy: 'ABAP:ZCL_DYN_FIELD_QUERY'

define root custom entity ZI_DYN_FIELD_ENTITY
{
  .selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Object Type'
  key ObjectType      : abap.char(30);

  .selectionField: [{ position: 20 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Policy Type'
  key PolicyType      : abap.char(20);
  
  @EndUserText.label: 'Table Name'
  key Tabname         : abap.char(30);

  @EndUserText.label: 'Field Name'
  key Fieldname       : abap.char(30);

  .selectionField: [{ position: 30 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Get Destructible'
  GetDestructible     : abap.char(1);

  .selectionField: [{ position: 40 }]
  @EndUserText.label: 'Field Values JSON'
  FieldValuesJson     : abap.char(1333);

  @EndUserText.label: 'Field Value'
  FieldValue          : abap.char(100);

  @EndUserText.label: 'Result Info'
  ResultInfo          : abap.char(200);
}

Explanation:

  • FieldValuesJson is used to pass dynamic field data as JSON
  • Output fields (Tabname, Fieldname, FieldValue, ResultInfo) hold processed results
  • The annotation @ObjectModel.query.implementedBy links to the ABAP class

Step 2: Create Behavior Definition (Unmanaged)

Define a behavior Definition for the custom entity:

unmanaged implementation in class zcl_dyn_field_query unique;
strict ( 2 );

define behavior for ZI_DYN_FIELD_ENTITY
authorization master ( global )
lock master
{
  field ( readonly ) ObjectType;
  field ( readonly ) PolicyType;
  field ( readonly ) Tabname;
  field ( readonly ) Fieldname;
}

Explanation:

  • Unmanaged behavior is used since logic is fully handled in ABAP
  • Fields are marked as readonly because this is a read-only scenario
  • No CRUD operations are required

Step 3: Implement Query Provider Class

Create the ABAP Class and implement IF_RAP_QUERY_PROVIDER:

CLASS zcl_dyn_field_query DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC.

  PUBLIC SECTION.
  " RAP Query Provider Interface for Custom Entity
    INTERFACES if_rap_query_provider.

  " Structure to hold dynamic field input from JSON
    TYPES:
      BEGIN OF ty_s_field_input,
        tabname   TYPE string,
        fieldname TYPE string,
        value     TYPE string,
      END OF ty_s_field_input,
      ty_t_field_input TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_s_field_input
                       WITH DEFAULT KEY.

  PRIVATE SECTION.
   " Result structure based on CDS Custom Entity
    TYPES ty_t_result TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zi_dyn_field_entity
                      WITH DEFAULT KEY.

   " Method to parse JSON payload into internal table
    METHODS parse_field_values_json
      IMPORTING iv_json      TYPE string
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_fv) TYPE ty_t_field_input.

   " Method to apply validation logic on dynamic fields
    METHODS apply_rules
      IMPORTING it_field_values TYPE ty_t_field_input
                iv_object_type  TYPE string
                iv_policy_type  TYPE string
                iv_destructible TYPE string
      RETURNING VALUE(rt_result) TYPE ty_t_result.

ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_dyn_field_query IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD if_rap_query_provider~select.

   " Variables to hold filter inputs
    DATA lv_object_type  TYPE string.
    DATA lv_policy_type  TYPE string.
    DATA lv_destructible TYPE string.
    DATA lv_json         TYPE string.
    DATA lt_result       TYPE ty_t_result.

    " 1. Extract filters from the Odata request
    TRY.
        DATA(lo_filter)      = io_request->get_filter( ).
        DATA(lt_filter_cond) = lo_filter->get_as_ranges( ).

        LOOP AT lt_filter_cond INTO DATA(ls_filter).
          CASE ls_filter-name.
            WHEN 'OBJECTTYPE'.
              lv_object_type  = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
            WHEN 'POLICYTYPE'.
              lv_policy_type  = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
            WHEN 'GETDESTRUCTIBLE'.
              lv_destructible = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
            WHEN 'FIELDVALUESJSON'.
              lv_json         = ls_filter-range[ 1 ]-low.
          ENDCASE.
        ENDLOOP.
      CATCH cx_root.
        " Optional: Handle Invalid filter scenario
    ENDTRY.

    " 2. Convert JSON payload into internal table
    DATA(lt_fv) = parse_field_values_json( lv_json ).

    " 3. Execute dynamic validation logic
    lt_result = apply_rules(
      it_field_values = lt_fv
      iv_object_type  = lv_object_type
      iv_policy_type  = lv_policy_type
      iv_destructible = lv_destructible ).

    " 4. Handle case where no valid input is provided
    IF lt_result IS INITIAL.
      APPEND VALUE #(
        objecttype      = lv_object_type
        policytype      = lv_policy_type
        getdestructible = lv_destructible
        fieldvaluesjson = lv_json
        resultinfo      = 'No valid JSON fields provided'
      ) TO lt_result.
    ENDIF.

   " 5. Handle total record count (for paging support)
    DATA lv_total TYPE int8.
    lv_total = lines( lt_result ).

    IF io_request->is_total_numb_of_rec_requested( ).
      io_response->set_total_number_of_records( lv_total ).
    ENDIF.

   " 6. Apply paging and return response data
    IF io_request->is_data_requested( ).
      DATA(lo_paging) = io_request->get_paging( ).
      DATA(lv_offset) = lo_paging->get_offset( ).
      DATA(lv_limit)  = lo_paging->get_page_size( ).

      IF lv_limit = if_rap_query_paging=>page_size_unlimited.
        io_response->set_data( lt_result ).
      ELSE.
        DATA lt_paged LIKE lt_result.

        " Apply offset + limit slicing
        APPEND LINES OF lt_result FROM ( lv_offset + 1 ) TO ( lv_offset + lv_limit ) TO lt_paged.
        io_response->set_data( lt_paged ).
      ENDIF.
    ENDIF.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD parse_field_values_json.
    " Return empty if no JSON is provided
    IF iv_json IS INITIAL.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.

    " Deserialize JSON into ABAP structure
    /ui2/cl_json=>deserialize(
      EXPORTING
        json = iv_json
      CHANGING
        data = rt_fv
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD apply_rules.

    " Loop through each dynamic field input
    LOOP AT it_field_values INTO DATA(ls_fv).
      DATA ls_result TYPE zi_dyn_field_entity.
      CLEAR ls_result.

      " Check if field exists in Data Dictionary
      SELECT SINGLE fieldname
        FROM dd03l
        WHERE tabname   = _fv-tabname
          AND fieldname = _fv-fieldname
        INTO (lv_fname).

      " Map input and context data to result structure
      ls_result-objecttype      = iv_object_type.
      ls_result-policytype      = iv_policy_type.
      ls_result-getdestructible = iv_destructible.
      ls_result-tabname         = ls_fv-tabname.
      ls_result-fieldname       = ls_fv-fieldname.
      ls_result-fieldvalue      = ls_fv-value.

      " Set validation result message
      IF sy-subrc = 0.
        ls_result-resultinfo = |Found: { ls_fv-tabname }-{ ls_fv-fieldname }|.
      ELSE.
        ls_result-resultinfo = |Error: { ls_fv-tabname }-{ ls_fv-fieldname } not in DDIC|.
      ENDIF.

      APPEND ls_result TO rt_result.
    ENDLOOP.
  ENDMETHOD.

ENDCLASS.

 

Result:

Field inputs given as a JSON payload can be dynamically processed by the service once the CDS Custom Entity and query provider logic have been implemented.

When the following request is made to the service:

/sap/opu/odata4/.../ZI_XXXXX?
$filter=
ObjectType eq 'BC_SFLIGHT'
and PolicyType eq 'DEMO'
and GetDestructible eq 'X'
and FieldValuesJson eq
'[{"tabname":"SFLIGHT","fieldname":"CARRID","value":"LH"},{"tabname":"SFLIGHT","fieldname":"FLDATE","value":"20260313"}]'

Conclusion

In this blog, we explored the challenge of handling dynamic fields in RAP when working with statically defined CDS parameters. We discussed a real-time scenario where field names, tables, and values are not fixed and need to be processed dynamically.

To address this, we implemented a solution using a CDS Custom Entity and passed dynamic input as a JSON payload, which is parsed and processed in the ABAP query provider class. This allowed us to validate fields against the Data Dictionary without modifying the CDS structure.

Overall, this approach provides a flexible and reusable solution for dynamic field handling in RAP, making it easier to adapt to changing requirements while keeping the design simple and maintainable.

entry.title: Handling Cross Business Object Transactions in RAP Using Actions
entry.content:
Introduction

In the SAP RAP, most examples focus on single Business Object (BO) operations. However, real-world enterprise applications rarely operate in isolation. A single business transaction often impacts multiple BOs—for example, creating a Delivery should trigger Billing, updating inventory, and possibly financial postings.
In this blog, we explore how to implement cross-BO interaction using RAP Actions, where:

  • A Delivery BO triggers creation in a Billing BO
  • Both operations are executed within the same LUW (Logical Unit of Work)
  • Data consistency is preserved using RAP’s transactional buffer

Business Scenario

Consider a typical Order-to-Cash process:

  1. A Delivery is created for a customer.
  2. Once the delivery is confirmed, a Billing document must be generated.
  3. The system should:
    • Automatically create a Billing record
    • Update the Delivery status to DELIVERED
    • Ensure both operations succeed or fail together

Implementation Steps

STEP 1: Create a Data base Table 

Data base  table For Delivery Details  

@EndUserText.label : 'delivery details' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 
define table zte_t_delivery_s { 
key delivery_id : abap.char(10) not null; 
customer_id : abap.char(5); 
material : abap.char(20); 
price : abap.char(10); 
delivery_date : abap.dats; 
delivery_status : abap.char(15); 
}

Data Base table for Billing Details

@EndUserText.label : 'Billing Details' 
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE 
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT 
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A 
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED 
define table zte_t_billing_s { 
key billing_id : abap.char(10) not null; 
customer_id : abap.char(5); 
delivery_id : abap.char(10); 
material : abap.char(20); 
price : abap.char(10); 
}

STEP 2: Create root CDS views for both tables

Root view For Delivery Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Delivery Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZI_T_DELIVERY_S as select from zte_t_delivery_s 
{ 
key delivery_id as DeliveryId, 
customer_id as CustomerId, 
material as Material, 
price as Price, 
delivery_date as DeliveryDate, 
delivery_status as DeliveryStatus 
}

 Root view For Billing Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Billing Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZI_T_BILLING_S as select from zte_t_billing_s 
{ 
key billing_id as BillingId, 
customer_id as CustomerId, 
delivery_id as DeliveryId, 
material as Material, 
price as Price 
}

STEP 3: Create Projection View 

  • Add UI Annotations
  • Add Action Button In Delivery Projection

Projection View For Delivery Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Delivery Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZC_T_DELIVERY_S 
provider contract transactional_query as projection on ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
{ 
@UI.facet: [{ id: 'deliverdetails',  
position: 1,  
label: 'Order Details',  
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE  
} ] 
@UI.lineItem: [ 
{ importance: #MEDIUM}, 
{ position: 10, type : #FOR_ACTION, label : 'Available', dataAction : 'create_delivery' }, 
{ position: 1, label : 'Delivery ID' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 1, label : 'Delivery ID' }] 
key DeliveryId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer ID' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer ID' }] 
CustomerId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 3, label : 'Material' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 3, label : 'Material' }] 
Material, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 4, label : 'Price' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 4, label : 'Price' }] 
Price, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 5, label : 'Delivery Date' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 5, label : 'Delivery Date' }] 
DeliveryDate, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 6, label : 'Delivery Status' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 6, label : 'Delivery Status' }] 
DeliveryStatus 
}

Projection View for Billing Details

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED 
@EndUserText.label: 'Billing Details' 
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 
define root view entity ZC_T_BILLING_S 
provider contract transactional_query as projection on ZI_T_BILLING_S 
{ 
@UI.facet: [{ id: 'Billingdetails',  
position: 1,  
label: 'Billing Details',  
type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE  
} ] 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 1, label : 'Billing Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 1, label : 'Billing Id' }] 
key BillingId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 2, label : 'Customer Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 2, label : 'Custoomer Id' }] 
CustomerId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 3, label : 'Delivery Id' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 3, label : 'Delivery Id' }] 
DeliveryId, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 4, label : 'Material' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 4, label : 'Material' }] 
Material, 
@UI.lineItem: [{ position: 5, label : 'Price' }]  
@UI.identification: [{ position: 5, label : 'Price' }] 
Price 
}

STEP 4: Define Behavior Definitions
 Delivery BO behavior

  • Enable CRUD operation
  • Declare a non-factory action

This Action is responsible for creating Billing data and updating delivery status

managed implementation in class zbp_i_t_delivery_s unique; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZI_T_DELIVERY_S //alias <alias_name> 
persistent table zte_t_delivery_s 
lock master 
authorization master ( instance ) 
//etag master <field_name> 
{ 
create; 
update; 
delete; 
action create_delivery result [1] $self; 
mapping for zte_t_delivery_s 
{ 
DeliveryId = delivery_id; 
CustomerId = customer_id; 
Material = material; 
Price = price; 
DeliveryDate = delivery_date; 
DeliveryStatus = delivery_status; 
} 
}

Billing BO behavior

  • Enable CRUD operations
  • use Late Numbering for auto-generating Billing Id
managed implementation in class zbp_i_t_billing_s unique; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZI_T_BILLING_S //alias <alias_name> 
persistent table zte_t_billing_s 
lock master 
authorization master ( instance ) 
late numbering 
//etag master <field_name> 
{ 
create; 
update; 
delete; 

mapping for zte_t_billing_s 
{ 
BillingId = billing_id; 
DeliveryId = delivery_id; 
CustomerId = customer_id; 
Material = material; 
Price = price; 
} 
}

STEP 5: Define Projection behavior 

Delivery BO projection behavior

projection; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZC_T_DELIVERY_S //alias <alias_name> 
{ 
use create; 
use update; 
use delete; 
use action create_delivery; 
}

Billing BO projection behavior

projection; 
strict ( 2 ); 
define behavior for ZC_T_BILLING_S //alias <alias_name> 
{ 
use create; 
use update; 
use delete; 
}

STEP 6:  Implement Behavior Logic

Delivery Class

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_DELIVERY_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler. 

PRIVATE SECTION. 
METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION 
IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_t_delivery_s RESULT result. 

METHODS create_delivery FOR MODIFY 
IMPORTING keys FOR ACTION zi_t_delivery_s~create_delivery RESULT result. 
ENDCLASS.
 
CLASS lhc_ZI_T_DELIVERY_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD get_instance_authorizations. 
ENDMETHOD. 

METHOD create_delivery. 
READ ENTITIES OF ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
IN LOCAL MODE 
ENTITY ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
FIELDS ( DeliveryId CustomerId Material Price ) 
WITH CORRESPONDING #( keys ) 
RESULT DATA(lt_delivery). 
LOOP AT lt_delivery INTO DATA(ls_delivery). 

"Create Billing Entry 
MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_T_BILLING_S 
ENTITY ZI_T_BILLING_S 
CREATE 
FIELDS ( DeliveryId CustomerId Material Price ) 
WITH VALUE #( 
( 
%cid = 'BILL1' 
DeliveryId = ls_delivery-DeliveryId 
CustomerId = ls_delivery-CustomerId 
Material = ls_delivery-Material 
Price = ls_delivery-Price 
) 
) 
FAILED DATA(ls_failed) 
REPORTED DATA(ls_reported). 

IF ls_failed IS NOT INITIAL. 
reported = CORRESPONDING #( ls_reported ). 
RETURN. 
ENDIF. 

"Update Delivery Status 
MODIFY ENTITIES OF ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
IN LOCAL MODE 
ENTITY ZI_T_DELIVERY_S 
UPDATE FIELDS ( DeliveryStatus ) 
WITH VALUE #( 
( 
DeliveryId = ls_delivery-DeliveryId 
DeliveryStatus = 'DELIVERED' 
) 
). 
ENDLOOP.
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

Billing Class

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_BILLING_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_handler. 
PRIVATE SECTION. 
METHODS get_instance_authorizations FOR INSTANCE AUTHORIZATION 
IMPORTING keys REQUEST requested_authorizations FOR zi_t_billing_s RESULT result. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lhc_ZI_T_BILLING_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD get_instance_authorizations. 
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lsc_ZI_T_BILLING_S DEFINITION INHERITING FROM cl_abap_behavior_saver. 
PROTECTED SECTION. 
METHODS adjust_numbers REDEFINITION. 
METHODS cleanup_finalize REDEFINITION. 
ENDCLASS. 

CLASS lsc_ZI_T_BILLING_S IMPLEMENTATION. 
METHOD adjust_numbers. 
SELECT FROM ZTE_T_Billing_S FIELDS MAX( billing_id ) INTO (ls_mat_num). 
DATA(lv_num) = ls_mat_num+3(4). 
LOOP AT mapped-zi_t_billing_s ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_material>). 
lv_num += 1. 
<fs_material>-BillingId = |BIL{ lv_num ALPHA = IN WIDTH = 4 }|. 
ENDLOOP. 
ENDMETHOD. 
METHOD cleanup_finalize. 
ENDMETHOD. 
ENDCLASS.

STEP 7: Service Defination

Expose Both Behavior objects

@EndUserText.label: 'Delivery and billing details' 
define service ZSD_T_DETAILS { 
expose ZC_T_DELIVERY_S; 
expose ZC_T_BILLING_S; 
}

STEP 8 : Service Binding

  • create service binding
  • Activate and publish

STEP 9: RESULT

Create a record and click on the Action Button the delivery Status will be updated and Billing records will be created 

Billing details when Action is trigged and Delivery status updated to Delivery

Conclusion

Cross Business Object operations in RAP are essential for building real-world enterprise applications. While RAP abstracts much of the complexity, designing such interactions requires a clear understanding of:

  • Transactional behavior
  • BO independence vs coordination
  • Framework-driven data consistency By leveraging RAP Actions and EML, we can safely orchestrate multi-BO updates without compromising on clean architecture or data integrity.

This example demonstrates how a simple Delivery trigger can drive Billing creation, showcasing a scalable pattern that can be extended to more complex business processes.

entry.title: RAP Unmanaged Implementation: Draft Enablement, Late Numbering, and Associations Explained.
entry.content:
Introduction.

SAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP) provides a powerful framework for building modern, scalable applications. While managed scenarios offer built-in handling of persistence and standard operations, unmanaged scenarios give developers greater flexibility to implement custom business logic and control the complete data flow manually.

In this blog, we will explore how to build an unmanaged RAP Business Object, focusing on key concepts such as Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item associations.

LATE NUMBERING:

           In Late Numbering the primary key (usually ID) of a business object is not generated at the time of creation, but instead assigned later during the save (persist) phase, this is typically handled inside the SAVE method of the behavior implementation class.
So initially:

  • Temporary keys are used during transaction processing.
  • Final keys are generated only when data is committed to the database.

Late numbering is useful in scenarios where:

  • Number depends on final validation or logic
    Example: Generate ID only after all validations pass
  • Avoid number gaps
    If numbering happens early and transaction fails → gaps occur. Late numbering ensures numbers are assigned only for successful transactions

How It Works (Conceptually)

  • User creates record → temporary key assigned
  • Data stays in transactional buffer

During SAVE:

  • Generate final number
  • Replace temporary key with actual key
  • Save to database

DRAFT:

Draft handling allows users to save incomplete data without activating it in the database.
It enables:

  • “Save as Draft”
  • “Edit Later”
  • “Activate when ready”

This is widely used in Fiori applications.

Why Do We Use Draft?

  • User convenience
       Users can partially fill data and come back later.
  • Avoid data loss
       Work is not lost if session ends.

How Draft Works (Flow):

  • User creates or edits data → stored in Draft table.
  • Draft data is isolated from active data.
  • User can:
    1. Edit draft
    2. Discard draft
    3. Activate draft

On Activate:

  • Draft data moves to active table.
  • Business logic (validations, determinations) is triggered.

Number Assignment in Draft with Late Numbering:
What happens to the ID in Draft?

When we use Late Numbering + Draft, the actual (final) key is NOT assigned in the draft table.

Instead:

  • A temporary key (UUID or %CID) is used internally.
  • The real number is assigned only during Activate (SAVE).

There will be two phase:
      Draft Phase and Activation Phase.
During Draft (Create / Edit).

  • No final number is generated
    RAP uses:
    1. Temporary key (%CID) in backend processing
    2. Draft UUID (technical key) in draft tables
    3. The business key field is usually:
          Empty OR Filled with a placeholder (optional, but not final)

So draft data is stored without the actual business number

During Activation (Save)

  • SAVE method is triggered
  • You generate the final number
    System:
    1. Replaces temporary key with actual key
    2. Moves data from draft → active table

SCENARIO:

In this application, we have a Product (Header) table (ZGN_T_PRODUCTS) and an Order (Item) table (ZGN_T_ORDERS) linked through productid. Users can create and save Product records independently, while Orders can be created later based on the product. The requirement is to generate both Product ID and Order ID only during final save (activation) using late numbering. Users should also be able to save incomplete data as draft and complete it later. To handle custom logic, and manual persistence, an unmanaged RAP approach is used

1.Database tables:

@EndUserText.label : 'Product table - Header'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_products {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;

}

 

@EndUserText.label : 'Order table - Item'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #NOT_EXTENSIBLE
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_t_orders {

  key client    : abap.clnt not null;
  key orderid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  orderstatus   : abap.char(10);
  location      : abap.char(20);

}

2. Created draft tables for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities to support draft functionality. These tables store intermediate data, allowing users to save incomplete records and activate them later.

@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_prdts {

  key mandt     : mandt not null;
  key productid : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid : sdraft_uuid not null;
  productname   : abap.char(15);
  quantity      : abap.char(3);
  price         : abap.char(7);
  totalprice    : abap.char(10);
  lastchange    : timestampl;
  "%admin"      : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}
@EndUserText.label : 'Draft table for entity ZGN_I_ORDERS'
@AbapCatalog.enhancement.category : #EXTENSIBLE_ANY
@AbapCatalog.tableCategory : #TRANSPARENT
@AbapCatalog.deliveryClass : #A
@AbapCatalog.dataMaintenance : #RESTRICTED
define table zgn_dft_order {

  key mandt       : mandt not null;
  key orderid     : abap.char(3) not null;
  key productid   : abap.char(3) not null;
  key draftuuid   : sdraft_uuid not null;
  parentdraftuuid : sdraft_uuid;
  orderstatus     : abap.char(10);
  location        : abap.char(20);
  "%admin"        : include sych_bdl_draft_admin_inc;

}

3. Created an Interface view on top of these two database table. Defined a proper association between Product (Header) and Order (Item) using productid to establish the relationship.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as select from ZGN_T_PRODUCTS
composition[1..*] of ZGN_I_ORDERS as _order
{
    key productid as Productid,
    productname as Productname,
    quantity as Quantity,
    price as Price,
    totalprice as Totalprice,
    lastchange as Lastchange,
    _order
}
@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order interface view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_I_ORDERS as select from zgn_t_orders
association to parent ZGN_I_PRODUCTS as _products
on $projection.Productid = _products.Productid
{
    key orderid as Orderid,
    key productid as Productid,
    orderstatus as Orderstatus,
    location as Location,
    _products
}

4.  Created a consumption CDS view on top of the interface views to expose the data for UI usage. Instead of using metadata extensions, all required UI annotations were directly maintained within the consumption view for simplicity.

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Product consumption view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define root view entity ZGN_C_PRODUCTS
  provider contract transactional_query
   as projection on ZGN_I_PRODUCTS
{
       .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Product details' },
                  
                  {
                  position: 2,
                  type: #LINEITEM_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details',
                  targetElement: '_order'
                  } ]
                  
    
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product Name' }]
    Productname,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Quantity' }]
    Quantity,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Price' }]
    Price,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 50 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 50, label : 'Total price' }]
    Totalprice,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 60 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Last change' }]
    Lastchange,
    _order : redirected to composition child ZGN_C_ORDERS

}
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Order projection view'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
define view entity ZGN_C_ORDERS as projection on ZGN_I_ORDERS
{
     .facet: [{ position: 1,
    type: #IDENTIFICATION_REFERENCE,
                  purpose: #STANDARD,
                  label: 'Order details' }]
                  
    .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 10, label : 'Order id' }]
    key Orderid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 20, label : 'Product id' }]
    key Productid,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 30, label : 'Order status' }]
    Orderstatus,
    .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
    .identification: [{ position: 40, label : 'Location' }]
    Location,
    /* Associations */
    _products : redirected to parent zgn_c_products
}

5. Created behavior definitions on top of the interface views for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) entities. Defined the required operations and enabled features like draft handling, associations, and late numbering as per our requirements.

unmanaged implementation in class zbp_gn_i_products unique;
strict ( 2 );
with draft;

define behavior for ZGN_I_PRODUCTS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_prdts
late numbering
lock master
total etag Lastchange
authorization master ( instance )

{
  create;
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly ) Productid, Totalprice;
  association _order { create; with draft; }
  draft action Activate optimized;
  draft action Edit;
  draft action Resume;
  draft action Discard;
  draft determine action Prepare;

  determination total on modify { field Price; field Quantity; }
  side effects
  { field Price affects field Totalprice;
    field Quantity affects field Totalprice; }

  mapping for zgn_t_products
    {
      Productid   = productid;
      Productname = productname;
      Quantity    = quantity;
      Price       = price;
      Totalprice  = totalprice;
      Lastchange  = lastchange;
    }

}

define behavior for ZGN_I_ORDERS //alias <alias_name>
draft table zgn_dft_order
late numbering
lock dependent by _products
authorization dependent by _products
{
  update;
  delete;
  field ( readonly : update ) Orderid, Productid;
  association _products { with draft; }

  mapping for zgn_t_orders
    {
      Orderid     = orderid;
      Productid   = productid;
      Orderstatus = orderstatus;
      Location    = location;
    }

}

 6. Implemented the required business logic in the behavior pool class using a helper class. This includes handling late numbering, calculations, and manual data persistence for both Product and Order entities.

    In the helper class, created a dedicated method to handle late numbering logic. This method generates the Product ID and Order ID and is called from the behavior pool adjust_numbers method.

 METHODS late_num CHANGING mapped   TYPE tt_late
                              reported TYPE tt_report_late  .

 METHOD late_num.
    IF gt_tab IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_products
      FIELDS
      MAX( productid )
      INTO (lv_max).
      LOOP AT gt_tab ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max>).
        lv_max += 1.
        <fs_max>-productid = lv_max.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

    IF gt_tab_item1 IS NOT INITIAL.
      SELECT FROM zgn_t_orders
     FIELDS
     MAX( orderid )
     INTO (lv_max_i).
      LOOP AT gt_tab_item1 ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_max_i>).
        lv_max_i += 1.
        <fs_max_i>-orderid = lv_max_i.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

     Called this method in the behavior pool class

  METHOD adjust_numbers.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->late_num(
      CHANGING
        mapped   = mapped
        reported = reported
    ).
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD save.
    zcl_gn_helper_unmln=>get_inst(  )->save_data(
     CHANGING
       reported = reported
   ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Implemented Persistance Logic for Header and Item.

Implemented the create methods for both Product (Header) and Order (Item) in the behavior implementation.

  • In the header create method (create_data), the incoming entity data is mapped to an internal table using CORRESPONDING, which is later used for persistence.
  • In the item create method (create_item):
    1. The Product ID from the header is captured.
    2. Item data is extracted using %target.
    3. A loop is used to assign the Product ID to each item, maintaining the header- item association.
    4. Finally, the data is prepared for database insertion and mapped back to the response structure.

    This ensures that all order items are correctly linked to their respective product during creation.

%target is used in composition (Header → Item) scenarios to access the child entity data (Item records) that come along with the parent during a deep create.

 METHOD create_data.
    gt_tab = CORRESPONDING #( entities MAPPING FROM ENTITY ).
  ENDMETHOD.

METHOD create_item.
    DATA(lv_pid) = entities[ 1 ]-Productid.
    gt_tab_item = CORRESPONDING #( entities[ 1 ]-%target ).

    LOOP AT gt_tab_item ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_item>).
      <fs_item>-productid = lv_pid.
    ENDLOOP.

    APPEND VALUE zgn_t_orders( productid = <fs_item>-productid
                               orderid = <fs_item>-orderid
                               orderstatus = <fs_item>-orderstatus
                               location = <fs_item>-location )
                        TO gt_tab_item1.
    mapped-zgn_i_orders = CORRESPONDING #( entities ).
  ENDMETHOD.

Output:

During product creation, the Product ID is not assigned initially and remains empty in draft mode; it is generated during the save (activation) phase through late numbering.

After clicking on create:

Same applies for Order ID, which remains blank during creation and gets generated upon save

The below image shows the draft table entries for Orders, where the data is temporarily stored during the draft phase. At this stage, the Order ID is not yet finalized, and the system maintains a draft UUID to uniquely identify the record.

After clicking on save the order id gets generated.

The below screen shows the active data after clicking Save (activation). Both Product ID (Header) and Order ID (Item) are now successfully generated using late numbering. The Product and its associated Orders are persisted in the database with proper header-item linkage via productid.

Conclusion:

In this blog, we explored how to implement an unmanaged RAP Business Object with key features like Late Numbering, Draft Handling, and Header-Item association. We saw how both Product (Header) and Order (Item) records can be created in draft mode and how their IDs are generated only during the save (activation) phase using late numbering. By handling the logic in the behavior pool and helper class, we achieved full control over data persistence, and associations.

entry.title: Sales Configuration display by CDS with table function/HANA SQL for Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP
entry.content:
In that blog I want to share approach to display Sales Item Variant Configuration in CDS-way. The approach could be used both in new reporting technology (like Fiori RAP-reporting (managed and unmanaged), Embedded Analytics) and in classical (ALV classic Dynpro, SE16N, SE16H etc.)

Technically, the approach is using the following:

  • Table Function with HANA SQL with scalar and table variable declaration
  • Functions STRING_AGG for row concatenation, CAST for casting to target type, CASE for conditional logic in HANA SQL
  • Window function for grouping and splitting the logic inside SQL-procedure
  • Sorting inside database procedure

So, it can be used both for Sales Item Variant Configuration and as a cheat-sheet for HANA SQL in AMDP-classes.

The blog is dedicated to Natalya Yakimenko; really encouraging and supportive person especially for Analytical Tools in Big Data / HANA.

What is Sales Item Variant Configuration?

In sales order we can parametrize item (configure) with characteristics according to customer needs: width, length, color, chemicals, mechanicals and so on. The configuration – is what the customer wants; so, it could be not only exact values, but interval values also; for example, Carbon (C) between 1 and 2 %. Also, it could be several values: for example, RED, YELLOW color.

On the Figure 1 it is shown sample of exact and multiple values. LENGTH has exact value and Colour has multiple values.

Figure 1 Exact value with UOM for LENGTH and multiple values for Colour

 

On the Figure 2 it is shown interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration. Carbon (C) has interval from 1 to 2 percents and Hydrogen (H) should be strictly more than 5% (also pay attention to different decimals part on the screenshot).

Figure 2 Sample of Interval values in Sales Item Variant Configuration

if you want more to know about variant configuration - you can check the links:

1) Introducing Variant Configuration ( https://learning.sap.com/courses/variant-configuration-in-sap-s-4hana-enterprise-management/introducing-variant-configuration ) (thanks to @Jelena_Perfiljeva  )

2) Variant Configuration (LO-VC) in SAP S/4HANA ( https://community.sap.com/t5/supply-chain-management-blog-posts-by-members/variant-configuration-lo-vc-in-sap-s-4hana/ba-p/14234012 )

3) Help-documentation on LO-VC component (https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_ERP/61155ee7ed304ae8b66b7526555a6a07/1a40b953495bb44ce10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US&version=6.18.latest )

How to read the variant configuration programmatically for reporting purposes?

Classical approach with function

In table VBAP (sales items) there is a special field (VBAP-CUOBJ, or CDS I_SalesDocumentItem-ProductConfiguration ) that links Sales Item Data Model to Variant Configuration Data Model.

For classical reporting very often the function VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE was used. It is still good function, and can be used, however

  • it does not allow us to select by characteristics values
  • it hardly can be used inside new approaches like Embedded Analytics and Fiori RAP-reporting
    DATA lt_CONFIGURATION     TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF conf_out.
    DATA lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR  TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF  api_value .

    CALL FUNCTION 'VC_I_GET_CONFIGURATION_IBASE'
      EXPORTING
        instance            = iv_cuobj                 
      TABLES
        configuration       = lt_CONFIGURATION                  
        et_conf_with_author = lT_CONF_WITH_AUTHOR               
      EXCEPTIONS
        instance_not_found  = 1
        OTHERS              = 2.
    IF sy-subrc <> 0.
      MESSAGE ID sy-msgid TYPE sy-msgty NUMBER sy-msgno
        WITH sy-msgv1 sy-msgv2 sy-msgv3 sy-msgv4 INTO sy-msgli.
    ENDIF.

 

For Embedded Analytics and RAP-applications I decided to create two group of CDS (view entities):

  • Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with specifying master info about characteristics itself: data type, single/multiple etc.
  • CDS with table function which present any char in the one line.

 

CDS with values in each Row

The CDS (view entity) for Sales Variant Configuration in row-mode with master data is the following. We could see that core tables are: IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The base part: 

define view entity ZI_CUOBJ_CLS300
  as select from ibin       as ibh
    inner join   ibinvalues as ibv    on ibh.in_recno = ibv.in_recno
    inner join   ibsymbol   as ibsymb on ibsymb.symbol_id = ibv.symbol_id
    inner join   cabn       as CABN   on  cabn.atinn = ibsymb.atinn
                                      and cabn.adzhl = '0000'


  association [1] to I_ClfnCharcDesc         as _CharDescr  on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID   = _CharDescr.CharcInternalID
                                                            and _CharDescr.Language           = 'E'
                                                            and _CharDescr.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'

  association [1] to ZI_CHARVALUE_MD as _CharValueH on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID =  _CharValueH.CHARCINTERNALID
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      =  _CharValueH.CHARCVALUE
                                                            and $projection.CharcValuestr      <> ''

{
  key ibh.instance                      as cuobj,
  key ibh.in_recno                      as IN_RECNO,
  key ibv.in_segmcnt                    as IN_SEGMCNT,

      ibsymb.atinn                      as CHARCINTERNALID,
      cabn.atnam                        as CharHure,
      _CharDescr.CharcDescription       as CharcDescription,
      cabn.atfor                        as dt_type,
      cabn.anzst                        as NumOfChars,
      cabn.anzdz                        as NumOfDecimals,
      cabn.msehi                        as char_uom,
      cabn.atein                        as Single_Mark,
      ibsymb.atcod                      as IntervalType,

      ibsymb.atwrt                      as CharcValuestr,
      ibv.dec_value_from                as CHARCFROMDECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.dec_value_to                  as CHARCUPTODECIMALVALUE,
      ibv.date_from                     as CharcFromDate,
      ibv.date_to                       as CharcUptoDate,
      ibv.time_from                     as CharcFromTime,
      ibv.time_to                       as CharcUptoTime,
      _CharValueH.CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,


      /* associations */
      _CharValueH

}

 

Part: ZI_CHARVALUE_MD

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'MasterData for Char Value'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI CHARVALUE_MD
  as select from I_ClfnCharcValue as ValID
  association [0..1] to ZI_CharcValueDesc as _ValDesc     on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID    = _ValDesc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber = _ValDesc.CharcValuePositionNumber
                                                                  and _ValDesc.Language              = 'E'
                                                                  and _ValDesc.TimeIntervalNumber    = '0000'

  association [1..1] to I_ClfnCharcBasic          as _ClasCharBsc on  $projection.CHARCINTERNALID     = _ClasCharBsc.CharcInternalID
                                                                  and _ClasCharBsc.TimeIntervalNumber = '0000'
{
  key ValID.CharcInternalID          as CHARCINTERNALID,
  key ValID.CharcValuePositionNumber as CharValuePos,
      _ClasCharBsc.Characteristic    as CharNamHuRe, --Human Readable
      ValID.CharcValue               as CHARCVALUE,
      _ValDesc.CharcValueDescription as CHARCVALUEDESCRIPTION,
      
      ValID.CharcFromDecimalValue    as CHARC_DECVAL,
      
      _ValDesc,
      _ClasCharBsc
}

 

part: ZI_CharcValueDesc

@AbapCatalog.viewEnhancementCategory: [#NONE]
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Characteristcis Value Desc'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@ObjectModel.usageType:{
    serviceQuality: #X,
    sizeCategory: #S,
    dataClass: #MIXED
}
define view entity ZI_CharcValueDesc 
    as select from cawnt as CharacteristicValueDesc
  
      association [0..1] to I_Language as _Language 
        on $projection.Language = 'E'
             
{
  key   cast ( CharacteristicValueDesc.atinn as atinn_no_conv preserving type ) as CharcInternalID,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.atzhl as CharcValuePositionNumber,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: '_Language'
        @Semantics.language: true
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.spras as Language,
        @ObjectModel.foreignKey.association: null
  key   CharacteristicValueDesc.adzhl as TimeIntervalNumber,
        @Semantics.text: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.atwtb as CharcValueDescription,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.aennr as ChangeNumber,
        @Semantics.businessDate.from: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datuv as ValidityStartDate,
        @Semantics.businessDate.to: true
        CharacteristicValueDesc.datub as ValidityEndDate,
        CharacteristicValueDesc.lkenz as IsDeleted,
        
        _Language
}

 

The result we could see like that (from SE16H).

We could get values, uom (unit of measure), and data type description for each characteristic from sales item variant configuration. We could use it for searching and data validations (during migration for example) also.

 

Figure 3 Sales Item Variant Configuration Sample

It is useful and informative, but it could lead to changes of cardinality in CDS-joins because we could have multiple values for some characteristics, also we need to do cast in each report/application and in case data type of characteristics is changed we need to change application. So, it would be better, for some cases, to get all values from one field. And it is possible with table function, as STRING_AGG is not working in CDS, but either in HANA SQL or ABAP.

 

Intervals could be like that (shown on figure)

 

Figure 4 Different types of intervals in Sales Item Variant Configuration

 

 

CDS with table function / amdp-procedure on HANA SQL

In table function we need do the following:

  • Format values (especially numeric ones) to their format from master data
  • Concatenate values into one field
  • For intervals it is needed to put special symbols: prefix, delimeter and postfix.

 

As in rows-oriented CDS it is using the following tables: VBAP, IBIN, IBINVALUES, IBSYMBOL, CABN, CABNT.

The idea is simple:

  • Target output type is CHAR with length 120 (of course can be longer, but for my purposes it was more than enough). (pay attention to the field ATWRT_STR in the table function below)
  • All values are selecting and casting to the target type depending on decimals in the characteristic’s master data.
  • If there are multiple values – it is concatenated in rows by function STRING_AGG
  • And for output purposes it is concatenated not only with values itself but with special sign (less (<), less or equal (<=), more or equal (=>), more) (pay attention to table declaration _t_interval_extstr and filling) (see figure 2 with Hydrogen value)
  • The procedure returns sorted data – so it can be used in applications for binary search purposes without sorting on the application’s side

 

Code sample for table function for sales variant configuration’s output

@EndUserText.label: 'SOI - Conf Cls300 - AGG mode'
@ClientHandling.type: #CLIENT_INDEPENDENT

define table function ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
returns {
  slsh : vbeln_va;
  slsi : posnr_va;
  MATERIAL: matnr;
  Plant: werks_d;
  ItemDescr: arktx;
  PRODUCTCONFIGURATION: cuobj_va;
  
  ORDERQUANTITY: kwmeng;
  OrderQuantityUnit: vrkme;
  
  ATINN : atinn;
  atnam : atnam;
  ATWRT_STR: abap.char(120);
  }
implemented by method zcl_tf_soicls300_agg=>read_cls300_one_line;

 

Code sample for HANA SQL AMPD-procedure

CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.

    CLASS-METHODS read_cls300_one_line
        FOR TABLE FUNCTION ZI_SOI_CLS300_AGG
      .

  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.

ENDCLASS.
CLASS zcl_tf_soicls300_agg IMPLEMENTATION.

  METHOD read_cls300_one_line BY DATABASE FUNCTION
        FOR HDB
        LANGUAGE SQLSCRIPT
        OPTIONS READ-ONLY
        USING vbap
              ibin
              ibinvalues
              ibsymbol
              cabn
              .


    -- https://help.sap.com/docs/hana-cloud-database/sap-hana-cloud-sap-hana-sqlscript-reference/
    -- ... table-variable-type-definition?locale=en-us

        declare _t_interval_extstr table(
            atcod "$ABAP.type( ibvalcod )",
            pre_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )",
            delim "$ABAP.type( char5 )",
            post_fix "$ABAP.type( char2 )"
        );

        declare _mandt varchar(3) := session_context('CLIENT');

* see data element  domanin: IBVALCOD
*0   Empty Quantity (No Value Allowed)
*1   = Lower limit
*2   [ Lower limit, upper limit )
*3   [ Lower limit, upper limit ]
*4   ( Lower limit, upper limit )
*5   ( Lower limit, upper limit ]
*6   ( - Infinite, upper limit )
*7   ( - Infinite , upper limit ]
*8   ( Lower limit, infinite )
*9   [ Lower limit, infinite )


        _t_interval_extstr =
            select '1' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, '' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '2' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '3' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '4' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '5' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '6' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '7' as atcod, '' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '8' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            union all
            select '9' as atcod, '>= ' as  pre_fix, ' - ' as delim, '' as post_fix from dummy
            ;


          _single_n_intervals =
        with _low_fromto_agg as (

          select
           ibval.mandt,
           ibval.in_recno,
           ibsym.atinn,
           cabn.atnam,

           cast( cabn.atfor as char(70) ) as atwrt,

           case cabn.atfor
              when 'CHAR' then
                 cast(
                    string_agg( ibsym.atwrt, '; ' )
                    as char (120) )

              WHEN 'NUM' then


*    begin of num - case (it consists of two parts: with intervals and strict equal)

               case -- ibsym.atcod
*    no intervals
                when ibsym.atcod = '1' or ibsym.atcod = '8' or ibsym.atcod = '9' then

                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                    when '0' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '1' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,1)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '2' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,2)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '3' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,3)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '4' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,4)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '5' then

                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,5)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    WHEN '10' then
                         cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (21,10)) ),
                            ';'
                            )
                          as CHAR (120) )

                    ELSE
                         cast( string_agg( ibval.dec_value_from, '+ ' ) as char (120) )

                  END

              else
*    with intervals
                 CASE cabn.anzdz

                   when '0' then

                        cast(
                          string_agg(
                            concat(
                                concat(
                                  concat( interv.pre_fix, cast( ibval.dec_value_from as dec (18,0)) ),
                                  interv.delim
                                  ),
                                concat( cast( ibval.dec_value_to as dec (18,0)), interv.post_fix )
                              )
                              , '; ' )
